skill.name: ai-avatar-video
skill.description: Create AI avatar, talking-head, and lip-sync videos through the RunComfy CLI using portrait images, audio files, scripts, and model-specific routing guidance.
skill.summary: Generate avatar and lip-sync videos with RunComfy model routes and CLI examples.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, URL, filesystem, and blocker patterns, but most command and blocker matches are Markdown examples or plain text, not executable Ruby or cryptographic code. Confirmed risk remains medium because the skill intentionally runs the RunComfy CLI, uploads user media to remote RunComfy services, stores an API token locally, and enables dual-use avatar generation that requires consent controls.

medium_findings.title: External RunComfy CLI Execution
medium_findings.description: Verdict: true positive for external command use. The skill provides multiple runcomfy run examples and an install command, which can execute a third-party CLI and process user-supplied prompt, image, and audio inputs. This appears intentional and scoped, not malicious.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and repeated in executable bash blocks. Risk is moderate because the command scope is documented as RunComfy-only and no arbitrary shell construction was found.

medium_findings.title: Remote Media Processing and Downloads
medium_findings.description: Verdict: true positive for network use. The skill sends image, audio, and prompt inputs to RunComfy model APIs and downloads generated outputs, which can expose user media to a third-party service. This is expected for the skill purpose and should be disclosed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill directly documents URL inputs, model API posting, polling, and result downloads. The endpoints are service-related rather than hidden exfiltration endpoints.

medium_findings.title: Dual-Use Voice and Likeness Generation
medium_findings.description: Verdict: true positive semantic risk. The skill creates speaking avatars from portraits and audio, which can be misused for impersonation or non-consensual synthetic media. The file includes consent guidance, but marketplace publication should keep a visible warning.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The capability is clearly described and the file itself acknowledges voice and likeness consent requirements. I found no prompt injection attempt or instruction to bypass consent checks.

low_findings.title: Local Token File Documentation
low_findings.description: Verdict: true positive but low severity. The skill states that runcomfy login writes an API token under the user config directory with mode 0600, and offers RUNCOMFY_TOKEN for CI. This is normal credential handling but should remain documented.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The local token path is explicitly documented, including restrictive file permissions. There is no evidence that the skill reads, prints, or exfiltrates the token.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: false positive for Ruby backtick execution, weak cryptography, and system reconnaissance labels. The cited lines contain Markdown inline code, model descriptions, tips, and exit-code documentation rather than Ruby execution, cryptographic algorithms, or host probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows plain Markdown prose and examples, not Ruby code or cryptographic implementation. No evidence of reconnaissance behavior was found in the reviewed file.

content.user_title: Create AI Avatar Videos with RunComfy
content.value_statement: Teams need fast avatar videos without manually choosing lip-sync models. This skill routes requests to RunComfy models and gives ready CLI steps for each case.
actual_capabilities: Selects a RunComfy avatar model based on audio, script, portrait, and scene requirements.
Provides CLI commands for OmniHuman, Wan 2-7, Wan 2-2 Animate, HappyHorse, and Seedance v2 Pro.
Explains when to use portrait plus audio, prompt plus audio, or prompt-only video generation.
Gives prompting guidance for talking heads, stylized characters, cinematic monologues, and short ads.
Documents exit codes, token handling, endpoint scope, and media consent requirements.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI and an authenticated RunComfy account before generation can run.
Sends prompts and media URLs to RunComfy services for remote processing.
Does not create or host source images, audio files, or consent records by itself.
Long scripts may need to be split into shorter clips for better synchronization.

use_cases.title: Create a Product Presenter Clip
use_cases.description: Turn an approved portrait and voiceover into a short vertical talking-head ad.

use_cases.title: Localize a Training Video
use_cases.description: Reuse one approved presenter image with different language audio tracks for localized lessons.

use_cases.title: Animate a Character Voiceover
use_cases.description: Choose the best route for a stylized character, mascot, or cinematic avatar scene.

prompt_templates.title: Make a Portrait Speak
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a talking-head video from this approved portrait URL and this voiceover URL. Use the simplest RunComfy route and explain the command.

prompt_templates.title: Choose the Best Avatar Model
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a script, a portrait, and no recorded audio. Pick the right RunComfy avatar model and prepare a short prompt for it.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multilingual Presenter Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a workflow for one approved presenter image and three language audio files. Recommend the RunComfy model and output organization.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Cinematic Avatar Scene
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Seedance-style cinematic avatar shot using a reference subject, reference voice, lighting direction, camera movement, and duration.

output_examples.input: A user provides an approved presenter photo and a clean voiceover for a product announcement.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends OmniHuman, lists the required image and audio inputs, and describes the RunComfy command to generate the video.

output_examples.input: A user has only a written script and wants a short social video with a speaker.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends HappyHorse, asks for concise quoted speech, and explains that the model generates audio during the video pass.

output_examples.input: A user wants an illustrated mascot to speak using an uploaded voice file.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends Wan 2-2 Animate and explains why it fits stylized character animation better than a photoreal portrait route.

best_practices: Use only portraits and voice recordings where you have clear rights and consent.
Prefer clean voiceover audio without music or background noise for better lip synchronization.
Keep each spoken clip short, then combine generated clips for longer narratives.
anti_patterns: Do not generate real people speaking without permission from the subject and speaker.
Do not pipe remote install scripts into a shell when setting up the CLI.
Do not place private media URLs or tokens in shared prompts, logs, or public examples.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses the RunComfy CLI and limits examples to RunComfy commands.

faq.question: Do I need a RunComfy account?
faq.answer: Yes. The CLI must be installed and authenticated before video generation can run.

faq.question: Can it work without a recorded audio file?
faq.answer: Yes. It can route script-only requests to HappyHorse, which generates audio during the video pass.

faq.question: Where are images and audio processed?
faq.answer: They are sent to RunComfy model APIs for remote processing and result download.

faq.question: Can it create videos of real people?
faq.answer: Only use it when you have rights to the person likeness and the speaker voice.

faq.question: Does the skill include safety guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents consent, token storage, endpoint scope, shell boundaries, and generated file size limits.
